evidence_quotes.quote: some publishers are taking away a product from people they sold it to by disabling video games and removing them from their customers' libraries making it impossible to ever launch the game they paid for, ever again.
there should be some way for that video game to be useable after that, be it offline (single player) or with help with so-called private servers (3rd party, fan-maintained servers).
There are hundreds if not thousands of examples of video games that were "killed" and lost forever, where people who bought them can no longer access them
they hide behind "it's not a product, it's a license for a service", but at the same time they never state any expiration date

paragraphs.text: Hello there, I am writing this comment with hopes that you could consider another glaring issue that plagues online ecosystem and that issue is that some publishers are taking away a product from people they sold it to by disabling video games and removing them from their customers' libraries making it impossible to ever launch the game they paid for, ever again. They hide behind "it's not a product, it's a license for a service", but at the same time they never state any expiration date, they use language typical for selling a product and then they just take it away. I believe it should not be legal for publishers to go into their customers digital libraries and remove purchased products same as it's not legal for car manufacturers to come to my garage and take away the car I purchased from them. It's completely fine for the publishers to end support and close their servers, but there should be some way for that video game to be useable after that, be it offline (single player) or with help with so-called private servers (3rd party, fan-maintained servers). There are hundreds if not thousands of examples of video games that were "killed" and lost forever, where people who bought them can no longer access them, where it's impossible to preserve them, or collect them, and industry is constantly pushing more and more into that rabbit hole with Ubisoft recently claiming that customers should "destroy their physical copies" if official support for the game ends. So I humbly ask that you look into those unfair and deceptive practices. It is very important issue that goes beyond video games and could be extended to all kinds of digital ownership. There also exists a European Citizens' Initiative called "Stop destroying videogames" which gathered over 1.4 million signatures, which clearly shows the massive support behind this idea and urgency of the issue. Thank you for your consideration.
